title: Simone Weil
summary: French philosopher, labor activist, and religious mystic who refused the distance between thought and suffering — leaving the lecture hall to work in factories, making attention and affliction the center of ethics, and pursuing God to the very edge of the Church without entering it, in a body of work published almost entirely after her early death.
hook: A brilliant young philosophy teacher took unpaid leave to stand at a factory press, on purpose, to learn what industrial labor does to a person from the inside. Simone Weil spent her short life closing the gap between what she thought and what she was willing to endure. The work she left behind, almost all of it published after she died, still unsettles readers because she tried to live every demand she made.
associatedWorks: The Iliad, or the Poem of Force

plainEnglish.theFigure:
Simone Weil was a young French philosophy teacher. In 1934 she did something strange. She took leave from her secure job and went to work in a factory. She stood at machines, drilled metal, and earned a worker's low wage. She was one of the sharpest students of her generation. She could have stayed in the calm world of ideas. She chose the noise, the danger, and the exhaustion of the factory floor instead.

Weil did this on purpose. She believed you could not honestly write about suffering from a comfortable chair. To understand the pain of workers, she thought, you had to feel it in your own body. This was the rule of her whole life. She refused to live better than the people she wrote about. That pattern damaged her health and shaped the circumstances of her early death.
plainEnglish.worldOfIdeas:
Weil was born in 1909 and grew up in Paris in a cultured home. Her family was Jewish but not religious. Her father was a doctor, and the house was full of books and talk. She was a gifted child and grew into a serious student of philosophy.

From early on, Weil cared about justice. She marched with workers and supported strikes. She read Karl Marx closely and agreed that workers were treated unfairly. But she also argued with Marx. She thought he trusted too much in big systems and revolutions. Real freedom, she believed, was about how each person was treated day to day. A factory could crush a worker's spirit no matter who owned it.

So Weil did not stay in books. In 1936 she went to Spain during its civil war to join the fight against fascism. Her brief service ended after she badly burned herself in a cooking accident and had to leave. Still, the pattern was clear. She kept stepping into the suffering she wanted to understand.
plainEnglish.centralIdea:
Two ideas sit at the heart of Weil's thought. The first she called affliction. This is not ordinary pain. Affliction is a crushing kind of suffering that hits the body, the social standing, and the soul all at once. A person in affliction feels broken and worthless, as if cast out of the human world. Weil saw this in tired factory workers and in the poor. She placed this suffering at the very center of how she thought about right and wrong.

The second idea she called attention. To Weil, attention meant emptying yourself and turning fully toward another person or toward the truth. It is a patient, quiet openness. When you truly attend to someone in pain, you stop putting yourself first. Weil thought this was the heart of both learning and love. Real prayer, real study, and real kindness all grew from the same deep act of attention.
plainEnglish.howSpread:
Weil also wrote about war and power. Her most famous essay studies Homer's old poem the Iliad. She called force the true subject of that poem. Force, she wrote, turns a living person into a thing, a mere object. The strong are not safe either, because force blinds them and sweeps them along. The essay reads like a warning written on the edge of the Second World War.

In her last years Weil turned toward God. She felt herself powerfully drawn to Christ. Yet she chose not to be baptized. She wanted to stay outside the Church, in solidarity with all the people it left out. She stood on the threshold, looking in, and would not cross.

Weil published very little while alive. After her death, friends gathered her notebooks and letters into books. Titles like Gravity and Grace and Waiting for God reached readers around the world. Her fame came almost entirely after she was gone.
plainEnglish.contestedInheritance:
Weil died in England in 1943. She was only thirty-four. She had tuberculosis, and she also limited how much she ate. Some say she refused to eat more than people living under German occupation could get. A coroner recorded her death as suicide. Many scholars reject that label. They point to her serious illness and say the truth is far less simple. Her death is still genuinely debated, and no single tidy story fits it.

This debate shapes how people read her. Some call her a kind of secular saint, a thinker who lived her beliefs with rare honesty. Others worry that her extreme self-denial was a sign of illness, not holiness. A third view holds both at once. Her life raises a hard question. Was her refusal to live easier than the suffering an act of love, a symptom, or some painful mix of the two? Readers still cannot agree, and that is part of why she endures.

beginner.theFigure:
In 1934 a young French philosophy teacher did something strange. She took leave from her secure job and went to work in a factory. She stood at machines, drilled metal, and earned a worker's low wage. Her name was Simone Weil. She was one of the sharpest students of her generation. She could have stayed in the calm world of ideas. Instead she chose the noise, the danger, and the exhaustion of the factory floor.

Weil did this on purpose. She believed you could not honestly write about suffering from a comfortable chair. To understand the pain of workers, she thought, you had to feel it in your own body. This was the rule of her whole life. She refused to live better than the people she wrote about. That pattern damaged her health and shaped the circumstances of her early death.

This debate shapes how people read her. Some call her a kind of secular saint, a thinker who lived her beliefs with rare honesty. Others worry that her extreme self-denial was a sign of illness, not holiness. A third view holds both at once. Her life raises a hard question. Was her refusal to live easier than the suffering an act of love, a symptom, or some painful mix of the two? Readers still cannot agree, and that is part of why she endures.

general.theFigure:
In 1934 Simone Weil, among the most admired philosophy students of her cohort, took unpaid leave from teaching and hired herself out to a factory. She worked at presses and drills, lived on a worker's wage, and kept a journal of what the labor did to her mind. The experiment nearly broke her health, and it confirmed her governing conviction: that suffering cannot be understood honestly from outside it.

That conviction makes Weil hard to place among the thinkers of her century. She was a trained philosopher who distrusted the comfort of the lecture hall, a political radical who criticized the revolutionaries, and a thinker drawn to Christianity who would not be baptized. She insisted on sharing the conditions she analyzed. The factory work, the brief turn to the Spanish front in 1936, the late self-denial — each was an attempt to think from inside an experience rather than above it.
general.worldOfIdeas:
Weil was born in Paris in 1909 into a cultured, secular family of Jewish background. She trained in philosophy at the École Normale Supérieure, where she stood among the strongest of her generation. Her early commitments were political. She organized with workers, supported strikes, and read Marx with care.

Her relationship to Marxism was both serious and critical. She agreed that industrial labor degraded the worker, but she rejected the faith that a change of ownership would cure it. Oppression, she argued, could survive any revolution, because it was rooted in the organization of work and power, not only in who held property. A planned economy run by a few experts could crush the individual as thoroughly as a private owner.

This is why the factory mattered to her as method, not just sympathy. By taking a worker's job in 1934 and 1935, she sought direct knowledge of how repetitive, supervised labor erodes thought and dignity. The notebooks from that year became the basis for some of her sharpest writing on freedom and necessity.
general.centralIdea:
Two concepts anchor Weil's mature thought. The first is affliction, her translation of the French malheur. Affliction is not pain alone. It is a compound condition that joins physical suffering, social degradation, and inward despair, and that strips a person of the sense of being human. Weil set this experience at the center of ethics. To respond rightly to the world, she argued, one must first be able to see the afflicted without looking away.

The second concept is attention. For Weil, attention was a self-emptying openness, a suspension of the grasping self so that reality and the other person could fully register. She made it the shared root of study, prayer, and love. To ask an afflicted person 'What are you going through?' and to wait for the answer was, for her, an almost supernatural act.

Her later notebooks add a stranger idea she called decreation: the willed undoing of the self that clings to its own existence, so that something divine might pass through the space left behind. The concept is difficult and easy to misread, and scholars still argue about how literally she meant it.
general.howSpread:
Weil's most widely read essay turned to Homer. In 'The Iliad, or the Poem of Force,' written around 1940, she argued that the true subject of the epic is force itself — the power that turns a human being into a thing. Force degrades the victim and intoxicates the victor, who is in turn swept toward ruin. Written as France fell, the essay reads as both literary criticism and a meditation on the violence of her moment.

Her final years brought a powerful religious turn. Weil described experiences she understood as direct contact with Christ, yet she refused baptism. She wished to remain on the threshold of the Church, unwilling to separate herself from the unbelievers and the excluded. This was a deliberate stance, not indecision.

Weil published little in her lifetime. After her death, editors assembled her notebooks, essays, and letters into the books that made her famous: Gravity and Grace (1947), Waiting for God (1950), and The Need for Roots (1949). Her influence is almost entirely posthumous, reaching readers across religious and secular traditions.
general.contestedInheritance:
Weil died at Ashford, in England, in August 1943, at thirty-four. She had advanced tuberculosis, and she had limited her food intake, reportedly out of solidarity with those living under occupation in France. A coroner returned a verdict of suicide by starvation. Many scholars regard that verdict as too simple, stressing the severity of her illness and the difficulty of separating her self-denial from her disease. The cause of her death remains genuinely debated.

The debate over her death mirrors a larger debate over her life. Some readers treat her as a secular saint, a figure of rare moral integrity who refused every comfort she could not share. Others read her extremity as evidence of illness, including a disordered relationship to food. A third position holds the two together and resists choosing. What is not in dispute is that her radicalism was lived as well as written. The unease she still provokes comes from that fact: she made demands that most thinkers only describe, and then tried to meet them with her own body.

advanced.theFigure:
The central interpretive difficulty in reading Weil is that her work resists the separation of doctrine from biography that scholarship usually requires. Her concepts were not propositions to be assessed in isolation but instruments forged inside specific ordeals — the factory year, the experience of physical pain, the encounters she described as contact with the divine. To read affliction as a free-standing thesis is to miss that she derived it, and tested it, against her own degradation at the machine. Yet to collapse the thought entirely into the life is to surrender the philosophical claims to pathology, which is precisely the reduction her sharpest readers have resisted.

This is why the framing of Weil as 'secular saint' — popularized in part by Susan Sontag's mid-century essay — is double-edged. The hagiographic frame honors the integrity of the life while quietly disqualifying the work from ordinary philosophical argument. Robert Zaretsky and others have pushed against this, insisting that her concepts of attention, affliction, and force be engaged as contributions to ethics rather than as relics of an exceptional soul.
advanced.worldOfIdeas:
Weil's quarrel with Marxism is the most analytically productive entry into her political thought, because it is not a rejection but an immanent critique. In Oppression and Liberty she accepts the diagnosis that industrial labor alienates and degrades, while denying the prognosis. Revolution, for Weil, does not abolish oppression; it relocates it. Power concentrated in a bureaucratic and technical apparatus reproduces domination regardless of the ownership of the means of production. The decisive variable is the structure of work and command, not the class that controls it.

This argument cuts against the grain of the revolutionary tradition she emerged from, and it anticipates later critiques of bureaucratic socialism by decades. Her factory experience supplies the evidential ground: what she recorded was not exploitation in the narrow economic sense but a methodical destruction of the worker's capacity for thought, a reduction of the person to a reactive instrument. Force, in her later essay on Homer, is the same mechanism written at the scale of war — the conversion of persons into things — which is why the political and the metaphysical strands of her work are continuous rather than separate phases.
advanced.centralIdea:
The hardest and most contested concept in Weil's late thought is decreation, and the difficulty is not merely obscurity but the suspicion that it sanctifies self-destruction. Decreation names the undoing of the autonomous, self-asserting 'I' — the created self that, by clinging to its own existence, occupies the space where God might otherwise act. To decreate is to consent to one's own nothingness so that the divine can pass through. Read uncharitably, this is a metaphysics of self-annihilation, and the proximity of the idea to her bodily self-denial and her contested death gives the suspicion real force.

The charitable reading, advanced by interpreters including Rowan Williams, distinguishes decreation from destruction: the aim is not the obliteration of the person but the dismantling of the egoistic will that distorts perception, so that attention can become pure. On this account decreation is the metaphysical correlate of attention, the same self-emptying described from the side of the will rather than of cognition. Whether the concept can be salvaged from its darker resonances, or whether its entanglement with her death marks a genuine danger in the thought itself, is the live question in Weil scholarship and the point at which interpretation becomes genuinely uncomfortable.
advanced.howSpread:
Weil's posthumous reception was shaped decisively by the editorial hands that produced her books. She published little in her lifetime; the canonical works are compilations. Gravity and Grace was assembled by Gustave Thibon from notebooks she entrusted to him, arranged thematically according to his judgment, not hers. The Need for Roots was edited from a manuscript written for the Free French. This means the 'Weil' most readers encounter is partly an editorial construction, a selection and ordering imposed after her death on material she left unsystematized.

The consequences run deep. Early readers, including T.S. Eliot, who introduced the English Need for Roots, received her as a religious thinker of striking purity, a framing the thematic arrangement of Gravity and Grace encouraged. Later scholarship, working from the more complete critical editions of her notebooks and the full record of her political writings, has recovered a more argumentative, more politically engaged thinker. The gap between the devotional Weil of the early anthologies and the rigorous critic of oppression in the complete works is itself a problem in her reception.
advanced.contestedInheritance:
The question her death forces is whether interpretive caution is honesty or evasion. The documented facts are limited: advanced tuberculosis, a refusal to eat in excess of occupied France's rations, a coroner's verdict of suicide, and the testimony of those who attended her. From the same facts, serious readers derive incompatible accounts — deliberate self-immolation in solidarity, the terminal phase of illness compounded by an inability to eat, or a religiously motivated asceticism that shades into something pathological. The evidence underdetermines the choice among them.

What is at stake is not merely biographical. To name the death a suicide is, implicitly, to read decreation as a doctrine that issued in self-destruction, and thereby to indict the thought. To insist on the illness is to protect the philosophy from its most disturbing possible expression. The most rigorous recent work — Zaretsky's, and the documentary care of Pétrement's biography — declines both reductions, holding that the cause is genuinely undetermined and that the philosophical claims must be assessed on their own terms regardless. The discomfort this produces is not a failure of the scholarship but an accurate registration of a life that refused to keep thought and suffering apart.

foundation.theStory: Simone Weil (1909–1943) was a French philosopher who insisted on living the conditions she analyzed. A brilliant student of philosophy, she left teaching to work in factories in 1934 and 1935, joined the Republican side briefly in the Spanish Civil War in 1936, and in her last years turned toward a stark Christian mysticism while refusing baptism. Understanding Weil means grasping why she thought ideas about suffering had to be tested in the body, and how that conviction produced both her central concepts and her contested early death.
foundation.theDebate: Scholars disagree about how to read Weil's life and death together. Was her extreme self-denial holiness, illness, or both? The coroner's verdict of suicide is contested by those who stress her tuberculosis. There is also debate about how much of the 'devotional' Weil is an artifact of how editors arranged her notebooks after she died, versus the more politically rigorous critic of oppression found in her complete works.
foundation.whyItMatters: Weil matters as a limit case for what philosophy can demand of a thinker. Her concepts of attention and affliction have shaped ethics, religious thought, and writing on labor and care. She also offers a sharp internal critique of Marxism — the argument that oppression can survive revolution — that anticipated later critiques of bureaucratic power. Studying her sharpens questions about the relationship between a thinker's life and the validity of their ideas.
questions: Weil believed you cannot honestly write about suffering without sharing it. Is this true? What does a thinker gain, and what might they lose, by trying to live the conditions they analyze?

Weil felt drawn to Christ but refused baptism, wanting to stay in solidarity with those the Church excluded. What does it mean to remain on the threshold of a belief on purpose, rather than entering or rejecting it?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The conversation on Weil divides along several lines. One axis concerns the 'secular saint' framing, popularized by Susan Sontag and resisted by readers such as Robert Zaretsky, who argue her concepts deserve engagement as philosophy rather than reverence as biography. A second concerns her religious thought: interpreters including Rowan Williams work to distinguish decreation from self-destruction, while others see in it a genuine danger. A third, more documentary line, anchored by Simone Pétrement's biography, concerns the facts of her life and the editorial shaping of her posthumous books by figures like Gustave Thibon.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence is unusually mediated. Weil published little; the major works are posthumous compilations arranged by editors, so the order and emphasis of her thought are partly not her own. Her notebooks, now available in fuller critical editions, allow a more complete reading, but they are working notes, not finished arguments. On her death, the evidence — advanced tuberculosis, restricted eating, a coroner's verdict, witness testimony — genuinely underdetermines the competing interpretations, which is why responsible accounts decline to settle it.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong argument about Weil must resist two easy moves: dissolving her thought into her biography (treating the concepts as symptoms) and severing the thought entirely from the life (ignoring that she derived affliction from her own degradation). The most defensible position engages a concept like attention or force on its own analytical terms while acknowledging the experiential ground she claimed for it. On her death specifically, the strongest position is to hold the cause as genuinely undetermined and refuse to let a contested verdict do the work of evaluating her philosophy.
questions: Weil's concept of decreation describes undoing the self-asserting 'I' so the divine can act. Can this idea be defended as a discipline of attention, or does its proximity to her self-denial reveal a danger in the thought itself?
Much of what readers take to be Weil's thought reflects editorial choices made after her death. How should the posthumous, compiled nature of her major works change the way we interpret her?

research.topic: The factory work as deliberate method
research.content: It is well documented that Weil took unpaid leave from teaching to work in factories near Paris during 1934 and 1935, including at Alsthom and Renault, and that she kept a detailed journal of the experience later published as part of her writings on labor. The factory year was not incidental but a chosen method for acquiring direct knowledge of how industrial labor affects thought and dignity. Her own notebooks and letters, together with the corroborating biography by Simone Pétrement, establish both the fact and the intent, and the resulting analysis informed her critique of oppression in Oppression and Liberty.

research.topic: The posthumous and edited character of her major works
research.content: Weil published relatively little during her lifetime; her best-known books are posthumous compilations. Gravity and Grace (1947) was assembled and thematically arranged by Gustave Thibon from notebooks she had given him; Waiting for God (1950) and The Need for Roots (1949) were likewise edited from manuscripts and letters after her death in 1943. Scholarship has established that the order and emphasis of these volumes reflect editorial judgment, so the 'devotional' Weil of the early anthologies is partly an artifact of arrangement. Fuller critical editions of her notebooks have since recovered a more systematic and politically engaged thinker.

research.topic: The cause and intent of Weil's death in 1943
research.content: Weil died at Ashford, England, in August 1943, at thirty-four. She had advanced tuberculosis and had restricted her food intake, reportedly in solidarity with those under occupation in France; a coroner returned a verdict of suicide by self-starvation. What evidence would resolve the question — a clear, uncoerced statement of intent, or medical certainty about how far illness alone determined the outcome — does not exist. The relevant records are the coroner's report, medical notes, and witness testimony, none of which can disentangle religious asceticism, terminal illness, and possible self-destruction. What can be responsibly inferred is that her self-limitation and her disease were entangled; what must remain genuinely unknowable is the precise intent behind her refusal to eat, which is why the bare label 'suicide' is contested.

comparativeNarrative.name: Religious and Mystical Reception
comparativeNarrative.content: For many religious readers, especially Christians, Weil is a figure of rare spiritual seriousness. They are drawn to her account of attention as a form of prayer and to her insistence that God is found in affliction rather than in comfort. What this vantage sees clearly is the coherence of her mysticism: her refusal of baptism looks not like doubt but like a costly fidelity to the excluded. What it can underplay is how sharply political and critical her thought also was. Read only as a devotional writer, Weil loses the hard edge of her analysis of work, power, and force.

comparativeNarrative.name: Radical and Labor-Critical Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: Readers coming from politics and labor see a different Weil: the activist who stood at the factory press and the critic who told the revolutionaries that their revolution would not free the worker. From this angle, her central insight is that oppression lives in the organization of work and command, not only in who owns the factory. This perspective recovers a thinker often lost in the religious frame. Its risk is the reverse error. Treated only as a labor critic, her later turn to God can look like a retreat to be explained away, rather than a continuation of the same concern with the human person.

comparativeNarrative.name: Skeptical and Clinical Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: A third group reads Weil with real unease. They notice that her extreme self-denial, including her restricted eating, can resemble illness more than holiness. From this vantage, calling her a saint risks turning a possibly disordered relationship to the body into a model to admire. What this perspective sees that admirers can miss is the danger of romanticizing suffering. What it can miss in turn is that a life may be both unwell and genuinely insightful. Reducing Weil to a diagnosis explains away her thought as easily as sainthood explains away her pain.

comparativeSummary: The argument over Weil is an argument about how to hold her thought and her body together. Each vantage reaches for a different Weil and risks losing the others. Religious readers prize the mystic and can mute the radical critic of work and force. Political readers recover the labor activist and can treat her turn to God as an embarrassment to be managed. Skeptical readers see the danger of admiring self-denial and can flatten a real thinker into a case history. No single frame holds her, because she refused to keep the philosopher, the worker, and the believer in separate rooms.

rabbitHole.label: Simone de Beauvoir
rabbitHole.reason: A near-contemporary at the École Normale and a clarifying contrast: de Beauvoir built a public life and a philosophy of freedom and self-creation, where Weil chose self-effacement and a philosophy of attention and affliction.

rabbitHole.label: Hannah Arendt
rabbitHole.reason: Another twentieth-century woman political thinker of Jewish background who confronted the same European catastrophe, and who, like Weil, made the relationship between thought, action, and atrocity central to her work.

rabbitHole.label: Karl Marx
rabbitHole.reason: Weil engaged Marx seriously and turned his own diagnosis against him, arguing that oppression lives in the organization of work and command and so can survive any revolution — an immanent critique that anticipated later critiques of bureaucratic socialism.

rabbitHole.label: The Factory
rabbitHole.reason: Weil's unpaid factory labor in 1934 and 1935 was her chosen method for knowing how repetitive, supervised industrial work erodes thought and dignity — the factory floor as the testing ground for her concept of affliction.

rabbitHole.label: Christianity
rabbitHole.reason: Weil's late mysticism drew her powerfully toward Christ while she refused baptism, choosing to remain on the threshold of the Church in solidarity with the excluded — a deliberate and influential stance within modern Christian thought.

rabbitHole.label: The Iliad
rabbitHole.reason: Weil's most widely read essay, 'The Iliad, or the Poem of Force,' rereads Homer's epic as a meditation on force as the power that turns a human being into a thing — a key text in the modern reception of the poem.

rabbitHole.label: Liberation Theology
rabbitHole.reason: Weil's insistence that God is encountered in affliction and that the suffering poor stand at the center of ethics prefigures later movements that placed the oppressed at the heart of Christian thought.

reference.author: Weil, Simone
reference.title: Gravity and Grace
reference.annotation: The aphoristic notebooks assembled posthumously by Gustave Thibon — the source of the concepts of decreation, attention, and affliction in their most concentrated form. Read with awareness that its thematic arrangement is Thibon's editorial choice, not Weil's own ordering.

reference.author: Weil, Simone
reference.title: Waiting for God
reference.annotation: The essential statement of her religious thought and her refusal of baptism, including the letters to Father Perrin and the essay on attention and the right use of school studies — the clearest account of why she remained on the threshold of the Church.

reference.author: Weil, Simone
reference.title: The Need for Roots
reference.annotation: Written for the Free French near the end of her life, this is her most sustained political work — the argument that human beings have a vital need for roots, obligations, and order. The book recovers the politically rigorous Weil obscured by the devotional anthologies.

reference.author: Weil, Simone
reference.title: The Iliad, or the Poem of Force
reference.annotation: Her single most influential essay, written as France fell — the reading of Homer's epic as a study of force as that which turns persons into things. The bridge between her political analysis of oppression and her metaphysics of affliction.

reference.author: Weil, Simone
reference.title: Oppression and Liberty
reference.annotation: Her most systematic political writing, including the critique of Marxism and revolution — essential for the argument that oppression survives any change of ownership because it is rooted in the structure of work and command.

reference.author: Pétrement, Simone
reference.title: Simone Weil: A Life
reference.annotation: The standard documentary biography, written by a friend with access to her papers and circle. It establishes the facts of the factory year, the Spanish episode, and the final months in England, and takes unusual care with the contested cause of her death.

reference.author: Zaretsky, Robert
reference.title: The Subversive Simone Weil: A Life in Five Ideas
reference.annotation: The best recent introduction organized around her central concepts — attention, affliction, resistance, roots, and the divine. Argues for engaging Weil's ideas as living philosophy rather than as relics of an exceptional soul, and resists the 'secular saint' frame.

reference.author: Gray, Francine du Plessix
reference.title: Simone Weil
reference.annotation: A compact, readable biography that is candid about the troubling dimensions of Weil's self-denial and contested death without reducing her to a diagnosis. A good first life for readers new to her, balancing admiration with unease.

reference.author: Williams, Rowan
reference.title: The Wound of Knowledge: Christian Spirituality from the New Testament to St John of the Cross
reference.annotation: Situates Weil within the longer tradition of Christian mysticism and supplies the charitable reading of decreation as a discipline of self-emptying attention rather than self-destruction — the key resource for the advanced layer's treatment of that concept.

commerce.title: The Subversive Simone Weil: A Life in Five Ideas
commerce.author: Robert Zaretsky
commerce.note: A first book for anyone who has never read Weil. It introduces her through five of her core ideas and her life together, and it treats her as a thinker to argue with rather than a saint to admire.

commerce.title: Simone Weil
commerce.author: Francine du Plessix Gray
commerce.note: Reach for this when the ideas feel daunting and you want the human story first. It is short, honest about the painful parts of her self-denial and death, and aimed at someone who wants the life before the philosophy.

commerce.title: Waiting for God
commerce.author: Simone Weil
commerce.note: The place to meet Weil in her own words. Begin with the essay on attention and the letters on why she would not be baptized — the most direct route into her religious thought for a reader ready for primary text.

commerce.title: Gravity and Grace
commerce.author: Simone Weil
commerce.note: For the reader who wants her thinking at its most compressed. Read it slowly and a few pages at a time, remembering that its order was arranged by an editor after her death, not by Weil herself.

commerce.title: Simone Weil: A Life
commerce.author: Simone Pétrement
commerce.note: For serious study of the life behind the ideas. Pétrement knew her and used her papers, making this the work to consult on the factory year, the Spanish episode, and the disputed circumstances of her death.

commerce.title: Oppression and Liberty
commerce.author: Simone Weil
commerce.note: Turn here to meet the political philosopher rather than the mystic. This is where her critique of Marx and of revolution is most fully argued, and it corrects the devotional image left by the popular anthologies.

popularCulture.title: "Attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity"
popularCulture.description: This line of Weil's travels widely online, on cards, in talks about kindness and mindfulness. It captures something real in her: that truly noticing another person is a gift. But the everyday version makes attention sound gentle and easy. For Weil it was a hard, self-emptying discipline, close to prayer, not a soothing habit.

popularCulture.title: The "secular saint" image of Weil
popularCulture.description: Many writers describe Weil as a kind of saint without a church, a thinker who lived her beliefs to the point of death. The image honors her rare honesty. It also quietly sets her ideas aside, treating her as someone to revere rather than argue with. Calling her holy can be a way of not taking her arguments seriously.

popularCulture.title: Weil and the anorexia debate
popularCulture.description: Because Weil limited her eating and died young, some later writers have read her through the lens of an eating disorder. This reading notices something the saint story hides: her relationship to her own body was troubling. But it can also explain her away. A person can be unwell and still think clearly, and a diagnosis is not the whole of a life.

popularCulture.title: Gravity and Grace as a quotation source
popularCulture.description: Weil's collected notebooks are mined constantly for short, striking lines that appear in essays, sermons, and social media. The fragments feel like pure wisdom. What gets lost is that they were working notes, picked and ordered by an editor after she died. Read alone, a line can sound like settled truth when it was really Weil thinking aloud.

popularCulture.title: "The poem of force" in writing about war
popularCulture.description: Weil's phrase for the Iliad, force as the thing that turns a person into an object, is borrowed often by writers on modern war and violence. It gives them a sharp way to name what battle does to people. Lifted from her essay, though, it can become a slogan, detached from the careful and unsettling argument she built around it.
